title: Razorpay Reverse Transaction Note Words
description: Razorpay shows merchants a transaction note like `"payment for invoice 42"`. The dashboard team wants to display the words reversed for a localized UI. Given a string `s` containing words separated by single spaces (no leading/trailing spaces), return a string with the order of the words reversed. Within each word, characters stay in their original order.

examples.explanation: Word order reversed; each word kept intact.

constraints: Single space between words; no leading/trailing space
hints: Split on space, reverse the array, join with space.
In-place: reverse whole string, then reverse each word individually.
Single word case: output equals input.

title: Swiggy Coupon Code Palindrome Check
description: Swiggy's marketing team launches palindrome-coupon promos: a coupon string is valid only if, after lowercasing and ignoring all non-alphanumeric characters, it reads the same forwards and backwards. Given a string `s`, return `true` if it is a palindrome under those rules, else `false`.

examples.input: s = "A man, a plan, a canal: Panama"

hints: Two pointers — left and right.
Skip non-alphanumeric on either side, compare lowercased chars.
Empty filtered string ko palindrome maan (vacuously true).

args: A man, a plan, a canal: Panama
tests.label: classic

title: Flipkart Product Code Run-Length Encoding
description: Flipkart's catalogue team compresses product codes with run-length encoding: each run of identical characters becomes the character followed by the run length (length only when length > 1). Given lowercase `s`, return the compressed string. If the compressed result is not strictly shorter than `s`, return `s` unchanged.

examples.explanation: 3 a's, 2 b's, 1 c → 'a3b2c'. 5 chars vs 6 — shorter, so return compressed.
Compressed would be 'abcd' (no runs); same length; return original.

constraints: s consists of lowercase English letters
hints: Single pass, count consecutive equal chars.
Append count only if > 1.
Compare lengths at the end.

title: PhonePe Length-Prefixed Message Codec
description: PhonePe needs to serialize a list of arbitrary user messages over the wire. Implement a single function `encodeDecode` that takes a list of strings, encodes them into one string using a length-prefix scheme (`<len>#<str>` for each), then decodes that encoded form back, and returns the decoded list. The point: round-trip must equal the input even when messages contain `#` or digits.

examples.explanation: Encoded as '5#hello5#world', decoded back to original.
Length prefix removes ambiguity from inner '#'.

hints: Encode each: length + '#' + string.
Decode: read digits until '#', take that many chars as the next message.
Empty list/empty string handle properly.

title: Group Shifted Cipher Strings
description: Two strings belong to the same shift group if you can rotate one's characters around the alphabet (mod 26) to obtain the other (e.g., 'abc' and 'bcd' — each char shifted by 1). Given an array `strs` of lowercase strings, group them by shift class. Return the groups, each sorted lexicographically, and the outer list sorted by each group's first element.
hints: Two strings belong to same group iff differences between consecutive chars (mod 26) match.
Build a tuple key: e.g., '1,1' for 'abc'.
Single-char strings all share key '' (empty tuple).

title: Zomato Find All Restaurant Anagrams in Menu
description: Zomato's search wants every position in the menu string `s` where an anagram of the query `p` starts. Return the list of starting indices in ascending order. (Note: this is the standard 'find all anagrams' problem; even though it can use a sliding window, the focus here is character-frequency comparison.)

examples.explanation: 'cba' at 0 and 'bac' at 6 are anagrams of 'abc'.

constraints: s and p are lowercase English
hints: Char-count of p, then compare to running count over a window of |p|.
26-length count arrays make compare O(26).
If |p| > |s|, return [].

title: Amazon SKU Longest Common Prefix
description: Amazon's catalogue groups SKUs by shared prefix (e.g., `'ELEC-TV-LG-55'` and `'ELEC-TV-LG-65'` share `'ELEC-TV-LG-'`). Given an array of SKU strings `strs`, return the longest common prefix shared by all of them. If no common prefix exists, return `''`.
hints: Vertical scan — char i across all words; stop at first mismatch.
Empty string in input → answer is ''.
Single string → answer is itself.

title: Implement strStr using KMP
description: Implement substring search: given `haystack` and `needle`, return the lowest index where `needle` first appears in `haystack`, or `-1` if it doesn't. If `needle` is empty, return `0`. The expected solution uses KMP (Knuth–Morris–Pratt) for O(n+m) — this is enforced via the largest test cases.
hints: Build the longest-proper-prefix-suffix (LPS) array of needle.
Use it to skip rechecking when a mismatch happens.
Empty needle → return 0 by convention.

title: Repeated Substring Pattern
description: Given a non-empty string `s`, decide whether it can be constructed by taking some proper substring `t` of `s` and concatenating multiple copies of `t` (at least 2). Return `true`/`false`.
hints: Length of t must divide length of s.
Try every divisor d of n; check if s[0:d] repeated n/d times equals s.
Slick trick: (s+s)[1:-1].includes(s) — but understand why before using.

title: Rabin-Karp Substring Search
description: Given `text` and `pattern`, return the index of the first occurrence of `pattern` in `text` using a Rabin-Karp rolling hash (with collision verification). Return `-1` if not found. If `pattern` is empty, return `0`. The grader accepts any correct answer — but the intended approach is the rolling-hash technique to handle large inputs in O(n + m) average time.
hints: Hash pattern; hash a sliding window of text of equal length.
Roll: subtract leading char's contribution, multiply by base, add new char.
On hash match, verify by string compare (collisions exist).

title: Roman Numeral to Integer
description: Given a Roman numeral string `s` (1 <= value <= 3999), return its integer value. Roman uses I=1, V=5, X=10, L=50, C=100, D=500, M=1000 with subtractive notation: IV=4, IX=9, XL=40, XC=90, CD=400, CM=900.
constraints: s is a valid Roman numeral in [1, 3999]
hints: Walk left to right; if current digit < next digit, subtract; else add.
Lookup table for letter→value.
Subtractive pairs are exactly the six listed.

title: Integer to Roman Numeral
description: Given integer `num` (1 <= num <= 3999), return its Roman numeral representation. Use the subtractive forms IV, IX, XL, XC, CD, CM where applicable.
hints: Greedy: list pairs (value, symbol) in descending order including subtractive ones.
Loop: subtract largest fitting value, append its symbol.
Repeat until num is 0.

title: Multiply Two Numeric Strings
description: Given two non-negative integers represented as strings `num1` and `num2`, return the product, also as a string. Do **not** convert the inputs to integers (they may exceed 64-bit). Implement long multiplication digit by digit.
constraints: No leading zeros except for the number 0 itself
hints: Result length is at most num1.length + num2.length.
Index i*j contributes to position i+j+1 (low) with carry to i+j.
Handle '0' * anything = '0' explicitly.

title: Add Binary Strings
description: Given two binary strings `a` and `b` (each contains only `'0'` and `'1'`), return their sum as a binary string. Don't use built-in big-integer parsing — do digit-by-digit addition.
constraints: a and b consist of '0' or '1' only
No leading zeros except for the value 0
hints: Two pointers from end; track carry.
Sum at each step = a_bit + b_bit + carry.
Append bit (sum mod 2), carry = sum / 2; reverse at end.

title: Integer to English Words
description: Convert a non-negative integer `num` (0 <= num <= 2^31 - 1) to its English word representation. The expected format: capitalized words separated by single spaces, with thousand-grouping words (Thousand, Million, Billion). E.g., `1234567 → "One Million Two Hundred Thirty Four Thousand Five Hundred Sixty Seven"`. `0 → "Zero"`.

examples.output: "One Million Two Hundred Thirty Four Thousand Five Hundred Sixty Seven"

hints: Helper: convert any 0-999 chunk to words.
Process num in groups of 1000; attach Thousand/Million/Billion.
Special-case 0 at the very top.

tests.expected: Twelve Thousand Three Hundred Forty Five
tests.label: thousands

tests.expected: One Million Two Hundred Thirty Four Thousand Five Hundred Sixty Seven
tests.label: millions

title: Valid Number Parser (State Machine)
description: Given a string `s`, decide whether it is a valid number. Allowed forms: optional leading `+`/`-`; an integer or decimal; optional exponent part `e` or `E` followed by an optional `+`/`-` and an integer. `'2'`, `'0089'`, `'-0.1'`, `'+3.14'`, `'4.'`, `'.9'`, `'-.9'`, `'2e10'`, `'+6e-1'`, `'53.5e93'` are all valid. `'abc'`, `'1a'`, `'e3'`, `'.'`, `''`, `'+-5'`, `'1e'`, `' '` are not. Return `true`/`false`.

examples.explanation: No leading/trailing spaces allowed in this version.

constraints: s consists of printable ASCII (no spaces accepted as valid)
hints: Track flags: seenDigit, seenDot, seenExp, seenDigitAfterExp.
Sign allowed only at index 0 or right after e/E.
Dot disallowed after exp or after another dot.
End: must have seenDigit, and if seenExp then seenDigitAfterExp.

title: Backspace Editor String Compare
description: An editor records keystrokes where `'#'` means backspace (delete the previous char if any). Given two such typed sequences `s` and `t`, return `true` if they produce the same final text. Since this is a strings problem (not stack-only), implement with simple string building using a stack/array.
constraints: s and t are lowercase letters and '#'
hints: Process each string into a stack/array; on '#', pop; otherwise push.
Compare final arrays.
Edge: '#' on empty stack does nothing.

title: Decode Bracketed Repetition String
description: Decode a string of the form `k[encoded]` where the bracketed part is repeated `k` times. Encodings nest, e.g. `'3[a2[c]]'` decodes to `'accaccacc'`. Input is guaranteed valid: no extra brackets, `k` is a positive integer, and original characters are lowercase letters.
constraints: s contains lowercase letters, digits, '[' and ']'
hints: Use two stacks: one for counts, one for partial strings.
On '[', push current count + current string and reset.
On ']', pop, repeat current string by popped count, append to popped string.

title: Count and Say Sequence
description: The count-and-say sequence: `S(1) = '1'`, and `S(n)` is generated by reading `S(n-1)` aloud — counting consecutive same digits and saying the count followed by the digit. E.g., `S(2) = '11'` ('one 1'), `S(3) = '21'` ('two 1s'), `S(4) = '1211'`, `S(5) = '111221'`. Given `n`, return `S(n)`.
hints: Build from S(1) iteratively up to S(n).
Helper: read off a string into count+digit pairs.
Output grows roughly by factor 1.3 per step.

title: Simplify Unix File Path
description: Given an absolute Unix path string, return its canonical (simplified) form. Rules: `'.'` is current dir, `'..'` is parent, multiple slashes collapse to one, the result starts with a single `'/'` and (unless it's just `'/'`) does not end with one. Names other than `.` and `..` are kept as-is.
constraints: path consists of English letters, digits, '.', '/', '_'
hints: Split on '/'.
Use a stack: push real names, pop on '..', skip '.' and ''.
Join with '/' and prepend '/'.

title: Camel Case Abbreviation Match
description: An IDE matches each query against a list of camel-case identifiers. The query may contain only uppercase letters that must appear (in order) as the uppercase letters of the candidate; lowercase letters in the query, if any, must also appear in order interspersed appropriately. For this simplified version: query consists of uppercase letters only, and a candidate matches iff the query is exactly the subsequence formed by all uppercase letters of the candidate. Given `queries` and a single `pattern` (uppercase-only), return a boolean array indicating which queries match.

examples.explanation: Pattern is uppercase-only in this variant; FoBa contains lowercase, treated as no-match here.

constraints: queries[i] is alphabetic; pattern is uppercase letters only or rejected
hints: Strip uppercase letters from each query in order; equal to pattern → match.
If pattern contains lowercase, return all false (per simplified spec).
Linear scan per query.

title: Razorpay Merchant ID Validator
description: Razorpay merchant IDs follow this format: prefix `'rzp_'`, then `'live_'` or `'test_'`, then exactly 14 alphanumeric characters (case-sensitive, `[A-Za-z0-9]`). Given a string `id`, return `true` if it matches the format, `false` otherwise. Do **not** use regex — implement the check explicitly with character/length comparisons.
hints: Check length first: 4 (rzp_) + 5 (live_/test_) + 14 = 23.
Verify prefix and mode segment.
Loop the 14 suffix chars; each must be alphanumeric.

title: Indian Phone & PAN Pattern Matchers
description:
A KYC service validates two formats and returns a tuple-like array `[phoneOk, panOk]`.

- **Indian phone** (`phone`): exactly 10 digits, first digit in `{6, 7, 8, 9}`. Optionally prefixed with `'+91'` or `'91'` (no spaces). Examples: `'9876543210'`, `'+919876543210'`, `'919876543210'`.
- **PAN card** (`pan`): exactly 10 chars, format `[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]{1}`. Example: `'ABCDE1234F'`.

Return `[isValidPhone(phone), isValidPan(pan)]`. Implement the checks explicitly (no regex).

examples.explanation: PAN last char must be uppercase.

hints: Phone: strip optional '+91' or '91' prefix; remaining must be 10 digits with leading 6/7/8/9.
PAN: length 10; positions 0-4 uppercase letters, 5-8 digits, 9 uppercase letter.
Return [phoneOk, panOk].

tests.label: phone non-digit, pan no last letter

title: BookMyShow Seat Code Parser
description:
BookMyShow seat codes follow the format `<row><number>` where `<row>` is 1-2 uppercase letters (`'A'..'Z'` or `'AA'..'ZZ'`) and `<number>` is an integer in `[1, 99]` with no leading zero. Given an array `codes`, return an object/dict with two fields:
  - `valid`: number of codes that match the format.
  - `maxRowSeen`: lexicographically greatest valid row prefix encountered (or empty string if none).

Return the result as `[valid, maxRowSeen]` to keep the JSON test format simple. Implement parsing with explicit char checks (no regex).

examples.explanation: All valid; row prefixes 'A','B','AA','C'; max is 'C' (since 'C' > 'B' > 'AA' > 'A').

hints: Walk each code: collect leading uppercase letters (1 or 2), then digits (1 or 2).
Number must be in [1, 99] and have no leading zero.
Track validCount and lex max of row strings.
